schools.summary: Gill: all the kings remaining, save those of Jericho and Ai already slain, now governing Canaan.
Keil & Delitzsch: the kings hearing thereof.

themes: when all the kings which were on this side Jordan... heard thereof
the report hardening the kings to war
a savour of death unto death (2 Cor. 2:16)

schools.summary: Gill: they gathered to fight with Joshua.
Keil & Delitzsch: them gathering with one accord, while Gibeon took another course.

themes: they gathered themselves together, to fight with Joshua and with Israel, with one accord
the union of the wicked against the church, yet vain
the kings of the earth set themselves against the LORD (Ps. 2:2)

schools.summary: Gill: Gibeon was a large and royal city, a metropolis with three others under it, perhaps governed by elders; they heard what Joshua had done.

themes: when the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done unto Jericho and to Ai
Gibeon choosing peace where the kings chose war
the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 9:10)

schools.summary: Gill: they acted craftily, with much cunning, in a different way from the other nations; took old sacks and old, rent wine bottles.

themes: They did work wilily, and... took old sacks upon their asses, and wine bottles, old, and rent
the craft of fear, a right end by a false means
we are not ignorant of his devices (2 Cor. 2:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the old shoes, worn out and patched with various pieces of leather.
Clarke: the sandals pretended to be worn out by long and difficult travelling.

themes: old shoes and clouted upon their feet, and old garments... and all the bread... was dry and mouldy
the studied counterfeit of a long journey
the prudent man looketh well to his going (Prov. 14:15)

schools.summary: Gill: the army had returned to Gilgal after Jericho and Ai, and there the Gibeonites applied to them.
Clarke: the request, make ye a league with us.

themes: We be come from a far country: now therefore make ye a league with us
the lie framed to fit the law's exception
leagues with far nations allowed, with the near forbidden (Deut. 20:15)

schools.summary: Gill: they did not yet know them to be Hivites, but so they afterwards appeared; the name signifies 'serpents'; they asked how a league could be made if they dwelt among them.

themes: Peradventure ye dwell among us; and how shall we make a league with you?
a godly wariness left half-pursued
prove all things (1 Thess. 5:21)

schools.summary: Gill: 'we are thy servants' was said in great humility, willing to do anything he should enjoin, though not meaning to be subjects; Joshua asked who and whence they were.

themes: We are thy servants. And Joshua said... Who are ye? and from whence come ye?
human probing unable to pierce a practised lie
if any lack wisdom, let him ask of God (James 1:5)

schools.summary: Gill: they magnified the 'far country' in stronger terms, careful not to name any country lest questions betray them; came because of the name of the LORD and his fame.

themes: From a very far country thy servants are come because of the name of the LORD thy God
true confession mingled with false pretence
error borrowing the language of truth (Matt. 24:5)

schools.summary: Gill: the two kings of the Amorites beyond Jordan, on the other side from Gilgal; they spoke of Sihon and Og.

themes: all that he did to the two kings of the Amorites... Sihon... and... Og
craft naming distant victories, hiding the recent
a false witness that speaketh lies (Prov. 6:19)

schools.summary: Gill: the elders and all the inhabitants of their country.
Clarke: all this, to the end of v13, was false, contrived merely to deceive Israel and save their lives.

themes: our elders and all the inhabitants of our country spake to us, saying, Take victuals
deceit fortified with invented circumstance
the heart is deceitful above all things (Jer. 17:9)

schools.summary: Gill: the words of the ambassadors to Israel, pointing to the bread they pretended was newly baked when they set out, now dry and mouldy.

themes: This our bread we took hot... but now, behold, it is dry, and it is mouldy
visible evidence deceiving the eye as a lie the ear
walk by faith, not by sight (2 Cor. 5:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the bottles, filled new on the day they came out, now rent.
Wesley: the bottles were leathern.

themes: these bottles of wine, which we filled, were new; and... they be rent: and these our garments and our shoes are become old
the cumulative weight of false proofs
the prudent are crowned with knowledge (Prov. 14:18)

schools.summary: Gill: the princes took of their victuals, not to eat, but to examine whether the bread was as mouldy as pretended; they asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD.

themes: the men took of their victuals, and asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD
the fatal failure to seek God's counsel
in all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths (Prov. 3:6)

schools.summary: Gill: the peace made on the princes' report that they had examined the provisions and found them as pretended.
Keil & Delitzsch: the league made and the princes swearing.

themes: Joshua made peace with them, and made a league with them... and the princes of the congregation sware unto them
a rash league bound by a sacred oath
he that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not (Ps. 15:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the league seems made the same day; the Gibeonites were in haste lest discovered, and Joshua and the princes took no pains to inquire; they heard the Gibeonites dwelt among them.

themes: at the end of three days... they heard that they were their neighbours, and that they dwelt among them
the truth discovered too late, the oath already sworn
be sure your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23)

schools.summary: Gill: not the whole camp, which remained at Gilgal, but a party who went to verify the report; their cities were Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kirjath-jearim.

themes: came unto their cities on the third day... Gibeon, and Chephirah, and Beeroth, and Kirjathjearim
the hidden nearness laid bare
bound to those they were charged to destroy (Deut. 7:2)

schools.summary: Gill: they smote them not, though they found the report true and that they were imposed upon.
Clarke: all the congregation murmured.

themes: the children of Israel smote them not, because the princes... had sworn unto them by the LORD... And all the congregation murmured
the oath binding even against fraud and the people's anger
thou shalt not take the name of the LORD in vain (Exod. 20:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the princes who went to Gibeon addressed all the Israelites there.
Keil & Delitzsch: the princes pleading the oath sworn by the LORD.

themes: We have sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel: now therefore we may not touch them
the princes holding the oath inviolable
he that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not (Ps. 15:1-4)

schools.summary: Gill: either this favour — preserving their lives — or the punishment of making them hewers of wood, was in their minds; they would let them live, lest wrath be upon them.

themes: we will even let them live, lest wrath be upon us, because of the oath which we sware
the breach of a vow provoking God's wrath
famine on Israel for the broken Gibeonite oath (2 Sam. 21:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the princes were pressing and importunate to save their lives because of the oath.
Clarke: 'hewers of wood and drawers of water' may be a proverbial expression for the lowest labour.

themes: Let them live; but let them be hewers of wood and drawers of water unto all the congregation
mercy joined with just consequence
spared yet appointed a humbler place

schools.summary: Gill: the Gibeonites, who came as ambassadors, were detained at Gilgal till Israel returned, and Joshua then charged them; he asked why they had beguiled Israel.

themes: Wherefore have ye beguiled us... when ye dwell among us?
the fraud rebuked even as the life is spared
rebuke a wise man, and he will love thee (Prov. 9:8)

schools.summary: Gill: they appeared the posterity of cursed Canaan, not escaping the curse, 'a servant of servants,' becoming servants of the Levites for the house of God.

themes: ye are cursed... bondmen, and hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God
the curse on Canaan turned toward the service of God
strangers that join themselves to the LORD, to serve him (Isa. 56:6)

schools.summary: Gill: they had often heard of it, not only certainly but frequently told.
Clarke: self-preservation: they were sore afraid of their lives.

themes: it was certainly told thy servants, how that the LORD thy God commanded... to destroy all the inhabitants... therefore we were sore afraid
a real if imperfect faith dreading God's power
by faith the harlot Rahab perished not (Heb. 11:31)

schools.summary: Gill: they were in his power and at his disposal, ready to submit to whatever might be enjoined.
Keil & Delitzsch: their surrender — do as seemeth good and right.

themes: we are in thine hand: as it seemeth good and right unto thee to do unto us, do
the unreserved submission of the helpless
let him do to me as seemeth good unto him (2 Sam. 15:26)

schools.summary: Gill: he did what was good and right, showing them favour and doing them justice.
Clarke: he acted according to justice and mercy, delivering them so the people slew them not.

themes: and delivered them out of the hand of the children of Israel, that they slew them not
keeping pledged faith and shielding even the unworthy
mercy and truth are met together (Ps. 85:10)

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua constituted them in this office; hence some think they had the name Nethinim, given to the Levites for the service of the sanctuary.
Keil & Delitzsch: their service for the congregation and the altar, in the place the LORD should choose.

themes: Joshua made them that day hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD
humiliation overruled to nearness in God's house
I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God (Ps. 84:10)
